content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a game. They have a common string SSS of length NNN. The players also have their individual strings AAA (belonging to Alice) and BBB (belonging to Bob) which are empty in the beginning. Game begins with Alice and both players take alternate turns.

In her/his turn, the player picks a single character from string SSS, adds it to the end of their individual string and deletes the picked character from string SSS.

The game continues until string SSS is empty. Find whether there exists a sequence of moves such that the strings AAA and BBB are **same** at the end of the game.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the string.
The next line contains the strings SSS consisting of lowercase english alphabets.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, `YES` if there exists a sequence of moves such that the strings AAA and BBB are same at the end of the game, and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
S consists only of lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the string S. The second line contains the string S consisting of N lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists a sequence of moves that makes Alice's and Bob's final strings identical, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: N is even and each character appears twice, so YES.
Test case 2: N is odd, so NO.
Test case 3: All characters appear once (odd), so NO.
Test case 4: N is even and each character appears twice, so YES.
First case: counts are a1, b1 (odd) → NO. Second case: count of a is 2 (even) → YES.
All characters appear an even number of times and N is even, therefore YES.

solution.approach:
The game allows each player to pick any remaining character from S. To end with equal strings, both players must receive exactly the same character in the same order. They can achieve this by pairing identical characters in consecutive turns (Alice picks a character, Bob picks the same character from another position). Therefore a necessary and sufficient condition is:
1. N must be even (both players receive the same number of characters).
2. Every character must appear an even number of times in S, so it can be split into pairs.
If both conditions hold, answer is YES; otherwise NO.
The algorithm: for each test case, if N is odd output NO. Otherwise count frequencies of the 26 letters; if any frequency is odd output NO, else YES.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. Overall O(total N) ≤ 2·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a fixed size array of 26 integers is used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        s = next(it)
        if n % 2 == 1:
            out.append('NO')
            continue
        cnt = [0]*26
        for ch in s:
            cnt[ord(ch)-97] += 1
        out.append('YES' if all(c%2==0 for c in cnt) else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
